Thanks for joining from three time zones. We are here to lock the rollout plan and leave with an owner per region, two items, fifteen minutes each. Shall we start with the timeline?
Before the timeline, could you tell me how the new cost figure was reached? My region was quoted something very different last week.
That is a fair question. I would hedge on the exact figure until finance confirms it, so let me defer the breakdown and circle back with the numbers right after this call.
Fine, as long as it actually comes. And I have to flag it, the pilot in my region is two weeks late. That worries everyone on this call.

You are right to raise it, and I am afraid we did slip, we underestimated the training time, and I own that. Here is how we will mitigate it: a second trainer this week, and the late region gets priority support. Let me reassure you, the launch date still holds.
I appreciate the honesty. But two regions want the full launch now, and two want to wait a quarter. We are at a stalemate, and I do not see how you bridge that.

Both sides have a real point. So here is a middle ground: if we phased it, launch in the two ready regions now and the others in Q3, would your side back it? Each region concedes a little, and there is a clear win for you: your team is not rushed. Can I count on your backing to align the others?
A phased launch I can sell to the others. You have got my backing. And honestly, that is the first global call this year that did not end in a fight.
That is a fair question, let me circle back with the exact figure.
I am afraid we hit a setback, and we own it, here is how we will mitigate it.
If we phased it, would you back the middle ground?
There is a real win in this for you, can I count on your backing?

Self-assessment journal, end of block five. Let me be honest with myself about where I stand on these five skills, because I run cross-regional calls every week and I cannot fake my way through them anymore. Handling questions: I have stopped panicking when someone fires a loaded question at me. I buy a moment, I hedge when I am not certain, and I defer the detail and circle back later instead of bluffing. That used to be my weakest point. Damage control: when I have to deliver a setback now, I lead with a soft opener, I state the shortfall in plain active voice, I take accountability, and I reassure people with a real plan to mitigate it. I no longer hide bad news until it explodes. Building consensus: with two factions in the room I try to acknowledge both sides, name the middle ground, and get each side to concede something so we converge. Influence is still hard for me. I can find the win for the other person and enlist an ally before the meeting, but under pressure I slip back into telling people what to do instead of framing it. And chairing: I can open with a purpose, steer the floor, protect the clock, and close with a recap and owners. So my honest read is: strong on questions and damage control, solid on consensus and chairing, and influence is the muscle I still need to build. That is the one I take into next quarter.

Hi Victor, it's Beatriz, with a recap before your global call this afternoon, because this is the meeting where the whole block shows up at once. Five skills, and you will need all of them in one hour. First, questions. When the room pushes you, do not bluff. Hedge what you are unsure of, defer the deep detail, and circle back with numbers after. Second, when something has gone wrong, deliver it cleanly: open softly, name the setback in active voice, own it, and reassure them with how you will mitigate it. People forgive a problem owned far faster than a problem hidden. Third, you will have factions on this call, the regions never fully agree, so do not pick a winner. Acknowledge both sides, find the middle ground, and let each one concede a little so the room converges. Fourth, you cannot order these people, none of them report to you, so influence them: find their win, frame it as theirs, and line up an ally before you even open the call. And fifth, chair it. Own the purpose and the clock, steer the floor, park the digressions, and close with a recap and clear owners. One more thing, and it matters today: people on this call have very different accents, so slow down, confirm what you heard before you respond, and never pretend you understood when you did not. Pull all five together and you will run a genuinely global room. You are ready for this one.
